source.book_title: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
source.note: Recipes from the southern parts of Kerala, particularly Trivandrum.

recipes.title: Ada Dosa
recipes.subtitle: A crisp, savoury breakfast pancake made from a medley of rice and grams
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Wash and soak raw rice and all the grams (black gram, split pigeon peas, split green gram and split bengal gram) in plenty of water for 3-4 hours.
Grind the red chillies first, then add the soaked mixture in small quantities to make a coarse batter; keep aside.
Grind onion, green chillies and curry leaves together to make a coarse paste.
Add this paste to the batter along with asafetida and salt; add water to get the desired consistency.
Heat the dosa pan and grease lightly with oil.
Pour a big spoonful of batter in the centre of the pan and spread with the back of the spoon to make a thin pancake.
When the dosa appears cooked on top, turn it over with a spatula.
Remove when crisp and serve hot with chutney.

recipes.title: Oratti
recipes.subtitle: A thick wheat and coconut pancake, traditionally served with a sunny-side-up egg
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Serve with fried egg (sunny side up / egg bulls-eye).

steps.instruction: Mix wheat flour, grated coconut and salt; add water gradually to make a very soft dough (consistency between chapatti dough and dosa batter).
steps.tip: The dough should be softer than chapatti dough — this is what gives oratti its tender texture.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan and transfer a small amount of the dough to the pan; spread the dough with your fingers to make a thick pancake.
Turn over a few times until the pancake is well cooked on both sides.
Serve hot with a fried egg (sunny side up).

recipes.title: Rasam
recipes.subtitle: A thin, fragrant, tamarind-and-pepper broth — the everyday comfort soup of a Kerala home
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Cook the split pigeon peas and tomato in a pressure cooker with 2 cups of water.
Grind red chilli powder, coriander powder, cumin seeds, garlic, turmeric powder and black pepper powder with a little water to a fine paste.
Transfer the cooked tomato and the peas (sauce only) by passing through a sieve into a saucepan. Add the ground masala, salt and asafetida. Bring to a boil and remove from heat.
steps.tip: Passing through a sieve gives rasam its characteristic thin, clear body.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan and add mustard seeds. When they pop, add dry red chillies and curry leaves and sauté for a minute.
Add this seasoning to the rasam and mix well.
Serve hot with rice.

recipes.title: Sambar
recipes.subtitle: The classic mixed-vegetable lentil stew that turns everyday rice into a meal
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Pairs with rice, dosa, idli, or appam.

steps.instruction: Cook the split pigeon peas in a pressure cooker with 2 cups of water.
Remove the cooker lid and add the cut vegetables, turmeric powder, salt and asafetida with 2 cups of water; cook on a high flame.
When the vegetables are cooked, add coriander powder, tamarind juice, okra and tomato and bring to a boil.
Add fenugreek powder and coriander leaves, mix well and remove from heat.
Heat oil in a pan and add mustard seeds. When they pop, add dry red chillies, curry leaves and thinly sliced shallots; sauté for a minute.
Add this seasoning to the sambar and mix well.
Serve with rice.

recipes.title: Nadan Chicken Curry
recipes.subtitle: A deeply spiced, roasted-masala chicken curry — the Kerala Sunday lunch classic
recipes.notes: This is the detailed home version with step-by-step photography. Serve hot with rice, chapatti, or appam. The roasted masala is what separates this curry from shortcuts — don't skip the roasting stages.

steps.instruction: Roast the dry red chilies separately in a dry pan. They're done when they swell up, become shiny, and start releasing smoke — remove immediately from the pan.
steps.tip: Chilies scorch fast and burnt chili is bitter. Watch for the shine and the first wisp of smoke — that's your cue.

steps.instruction: Lower the heat and roast the coriander seeds in the same pan until fragrant and golden brown.
steps.tip: You'll smell it before you see it — coriander goes from raw to fragrant to golden in under a minute on low heat.

steps.instruction: Lower the heat further and lightly roast the whole spices (fennel, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise) for less than a minute — just enough to dry them out.
steps.tip: Whole spices need only seconds. Over-roasting makes them harsh.

steps.instruction: Remove the stems from the roasted red chillies. Grind all the roasted ingredients together to a fine powder.
Thinly slice the onions.
Finely chop the green chillies, ginger and garlic together.
Heat oil in a heavy pan and add the sliced onions.
When the onions are deeply browned, add the chopped green chilli, ginger and garlic. Sauté for a couple of minutes.
steps.tip: Patience here is everything. Brown onions — not golden, properly brown — are the foundation of this curry's flavour.

steps.instruction: Add the chicken pieces and sauté on high heat until the flesh turns white.
Add salt and the ground roasted masala powder. Mix thoroughly so every piece of chicken is coated.
Add half to one cup of hot water to the chicken and mix well.
steps.tip: Use hot, not cold, water — it keeps the chicken tender and the masala from seizing.

steps.instruction: Lower the heat to medium, cover the pan, and cook for about 5 minutes.
Add the potato cubes (optional), cover, and cook for about 10 minutes.
steps.tip: Potatoes are traditional but optional — they soak up the gravy beautifully.

steps.instruction: Prepare the coconut milk by stirring 3–4 heaped tablespoons of coconut milk powder into 1 cup of hot water. Add the coconut milk to the chicken through a strainer.
steps.tip: Straining catches any lumps and gives a smoother gravy.

steps.instruction: Keep the pan uncovered and continue cooking until the potatoes are tender and the gravy has reduced to the right consistency. Check salt and adjust if needed. Switch off the flame.
steps.tip: "Right consistency" is a matter of taste — somewhere between a thick gravy and a stew.

steps.instruction: Prepare the ingredients for the seasoning (tadka). Slice the shallots as shown.
Heat 2 tablespoons of coconut oil in a small seasoning pan. When the oil is hot, add mustard seeds. When they pop completely, add the sliced shallots and brown them.
steps.tip: Wait for the mustard seeds to finish popping before adding shallots — the sound tells you when.

steps.instruction: When the shallots are brown, add the curry leaves and dry red chillies. Sauté for a few seconds.
steps.tip: Curry leaves go in last — they crackle and release their aroma in seconds.

steps.instruction: Pour the sizzling seasoning over the chicken curry. Cover the pan immediately and keep it covered for a few seconds so the aromatic steam is trapped in.
steps.tip: Trapping the tadka steam is an old Kerala trick — it lets the aroma settle into the curry rather than escaping into the kitchen.

steps.instruction: Transfer the chicken curry to a serving plate.

recipes.title: Chicken Stew
recipes.subtitle: A gentle, peppery, coconut-milk chicken stew — the traditional partner for appam
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Serve with appam. You can replace the chicken with mutton.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan and add onions, ginger, garlic and green chillies; sauté well.
Add black pepper powder and garam masala, followed by the tomatoes; sauté until the oil separates.
Add the cut chicken and salt with 1 cup of water; cook over a low flame.
When the chicken is about three-quarters done, add the potatoes.
When the chicken and potatoes are cooked and the gravy is reduced to the required consistency, add the coconut milk and curry leaves; bring to a boil.
steps.tip: Don't let coconut milk boil too long — add it at the end and bring to a gentle boil to keep it from splitting.

steps.instruction: Serve with appam.

recipes.title: Chicken Purattiathu
recipes.subtitle: A dry, deeply browned chicken fry with caramelised onions — rich and restaurant-style at home
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan and add half of the sliced onions and green chillies; sauté for a few minutes.
Add coriander powder, red chilli powder, turmeric powder and garam masala; sauté until the raw smell disappears.
Add the chicken and salt and cook over a low flame. You may add a little water if required. Cook until the water has evaporated.
In a separate pan, heat oil and add the other half of the sliced onions; sauté until the onion turns golden brown.
steps.tip: Patience here is everything — cook the second batch of onions until deeply golden, not just translucent.

steps.instruction: Add the cooked chicken and stir-fry well for 5-10 minutes. Add curry leaves and mix well.
Serve hot with rice.

recipes.title: Meen Curry
recipes.subtitle: Kerala's iconic tangy, coconut-based fish curry — best the day after it's made
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Add drumstick and raw mango when making tuna curry. Tastes even better the next day as the flavours settle.

ingredients.name: Fish (mackerel, sardines or tuna)
ingredients.preparation: cleaned and cut into required-size pieces

steps.instruction: Clean and cut the fish into required-size pieces.
In a pan, roast the coriander seeds and dry red chillies well.
Grind the roasted coriander seeds and dry red chillies to a fine paste with a little water.
Add grated coconut and shallots and grind further to make a coarse paste.
In a pan, add the ground masala to the fish. Add turmeric powder, fenugreek powder, tamarind juice, green chillies, curry leaves, salt and sufficient water; cook.
steps.tip: A traditional earthen 'manchatti' pot gives this curry the best flavour.

steps.instruction: When the fish is cooked and the gravy is reduced, add coconut oil and curry leaves; mix well.
Serve hot with rice.

recipes.title: Thenga Arakkatha Meen Curry
recipes.subtitle: A coconut-free red fish curry, sharp with gambooge and a hit of raw coconut oil at the end
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Serve hot with rice. 'Arakkatha' means ground-coconut-free — this curry is lighter and keeps longer.

ingredients.name: Fish (kingfish)
ingredients.preparation: cleaned and cut into required-size pieces

steps.instruction: Clean and cut the fish into required-size pieces.
Heat oil in a pan and add shallots, garlic and ginger; sauté well.
Add red chilli powder and turmeric powder and sauté on a low flame for a minute.
steps.tip: Keep the flame low — chilli powder burns easily and turns bitter.

steps.instruction: Add 2 cups of water, gambooge, curry leaves, fenugreek powder and salt; bring to a boil.
Add the fish pieces and continue boiling for 3-4 minutes.
Lower the heat and reduce the sauce. Once the fish is cooked, add coconut oil and curry leaves.
steps.tip: The raw coconut oil drizzled at the end is non-negotiable — it's the signature finish.

steps.instruction: Mix well and serve hot with rice.

recipes.title: Mutta Roast
recipes.subtitle: Boiled eggs smothered in a thick, onion-heavy roasted masala — the perfect appam companion
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Boil the eggs, remove the shells, and keep aside.
Heat oil in a pan; add onion, green chillies, garlic and ginger; sauté until the onions turn light brown.
Add the chopped tomatoes and sauté further until the oil separates.
Add coriander powder, red chilli powder, turmeric powder, garam masala, ½ cup of water and salt; mix well.
Cook on a low flame and remove when the sauce has a very thick consistency.
steps.tip: The masala should coat a spoon — nearly dry, not saucy. That's what makes it a 'roast'.

steps.instruction: Remove from heat, add coriander leaves and the boiled eggs; mix to coat the eggs with the masala.
Serve with appam.

recipes.title: Nadan Scrambled Eggs
recipes.subtitle: A quick, peppery Kerala-style scramble with curry leaves and mustard seeds
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Crack the eggs into a mixing bowl, add salt, and whisk to make an even mixture.
Heat oil in a frying pan and add mustard seeds. When they pop, add onion, green chillies, dry red chilli and curry leaves; sauté for 2 minutes.
Add the egg mixture and cook on medium heat, folding the mixture frequently to scramble.
steps.tip: Fold gently and slowly — it's the difference between creamy curds and rubbery lumps.

steps.instruction: Serve with bread or rice.

recipes.title: Ghee Rice
recipes.subtitle: Fragrant basmati perfumed with whole spices and ghee — a family feast centrepiece
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Wash and soak the rice for half an hour; drain and keep aside.
Heat ghee in a deep pan and add cardamom, cloves and cinnamon; sauté for a minute.
Add the chopped onions and sauté for another minute.
Add rice, mix well and sauté for 4 minutes.
steps.tip: Toasting the rice in ghee before adding water keeps the grains separate and aromatic.

steps.instruction: Add double the volume of water and salt; bring to a boil.
Reduce the heat, cover and cook over a low flame for 15 minutes, or until the water has evaporated.
Serve with chicken curry.

recipes.title: Vegetable Pilau
recipes.subtitle: An everyday one-pot rice with carrot and beans — plain enough for a weeknight, dressed enough for guests
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Serve with chicken curry or any gravy of choice.

steps.instruction: Wash and soak the rice for half an hour; drain and keep aside.
Heat oil in a deep pan; add the onion and sauté for 3 minutes.
Add ginger and garlic paste; sauté until the raw smell disappears.
Add the vegetables and sauté for a further 3 minutes.
Add rice, mix well and sauté for 4 minutes.
Add double the volume of water and salt; bring to a boil.
Reduce the heat, cover and cook over a low flame for 15 minutes, or until the water has evaporated.
Garnish with coriander leaves and serve with chicken curry.

recipes.title: Pakkavada
recipes.subtitle: Crisp, spiraled rice-flour savouries pressed through a 'seva nazhi' — tea-time essentials
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection
recipes.notes: Serve with hot tea. A 'seva nazhi' (pakkavada press/sieve) is needed to shape the dough.

steps.instruction: Mix the raw rice powder, gram flour, red chilli powder, asafetida powder, salt and butter. Gradually add water and knead to a soft dough that can be pressed through the seva nazhi.
Heat oil in a wok or kadai.
Fill the seva nazhi with the mixture and press it directly in a circular motion over the whole surface of the hot oil.
steps.tip: Press in one continuous spiral — don't lift between strokes or the pakkavada breaks into pieces.

steps.instruction: Fry the pakkavada until it turns golden brown.
Remove and drain on paper napkins.
Serve with hot tea.

recipes.title: Ulli Bhaji
recipes.subtitle: Crisp onion fritters in a spiced gram-flour batter — the rain-and-chai snack
recipes.source: Good food at home — A Kerala Family Recipe Collection

steps.instruction: Mix gram flour, red chilli powder, asafetida and salt. Add water gradually to make a batter of a consistency that will evenly coat the sliced onions.
Heat coconut oil in a deep wok (kadai).
Dunk the onion slices into the batter to coat them evenly.
Slide the coated onion pieces with a spoon into the hot oil and deep-fry until golden brown.
steps.tip: Coconut oil is worth it — regular oil works, but the flavour won't be the same.

steps.instruction: Remove and drain on paper napkins.
Serve with hot tea.
